calendar.matters:
The Regents Of California Notice Of Motion And Motion To Dismiss Plaintiff Petition For Writ Of Mandate And Complaint
Off Calendar Feb-19-2021 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
William Eliopoulos (650-320-1500; weliopoulos@rutan.com) and Zeddie Suggs of Rutan & Tucker LLP for Petitioner, Redwood Electric Group, Inc.
Daniel Sakaguchi (415.623.7016; dsakaguchi@vlmglaw.com) and Brooke Anderson of Varela Lee Metz & Guarino LLP for Respondents, The Regents of the University of California.

ARGUED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument adopts the tentative ruling as follows:

Respondent The Regents Of California's Motion To Dismiss Plaintiff's Petition For Writ Of Mandate And Complaint:

Respondent ("The Regents") motion to dismiss is denied. The Regents fails to demonstrate that the case is moot.

While potential stigma alone does not prevent a finding of mootness, the collateral consequences from the hearing officer's determination that petitioner was properly substituted out of the project show that a live controversy still exists. (See In re Cassandra B. (2004) 125 Cal.App.4th 199, 209-210 [appeal from restraining order not moot based on collateral consequences of order remaining on file]; Doe v. Allee (2019) 30 Cal.App.5th 1036, 1059 [student's writ to reverse finding that he committed sex offense was not moot because reputational damage infringed on future employment opportunities] .) Here, petitioner demonstrates that the existence of the substitution determination will adversely affect its future employment prospects. (Castello Decl., pars. 2-4.) The case is therefore not moot.

In addition, the case is not moot because the court must address the merits to determine the propriety of petitioner's fee request. (See Indio Police Command Unit Assn. v. City of Indio (2014) 230 Cal.App.4th 521 [case not moot where finding of mootness would determine propriety of fee award]; Vargas v. Balz (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 1544, 1551 [same].)

order is signed.

Judge: Richard Ulmer, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/RBU)

DEMURRER TO PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE/ PROHIBITION/ CERTIFICATION
Off Calendar Dec-17-2019 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES: Appearances:
Carrie Macintosh, Esq. 650-320-1500 of Rutan & Tucker for Plaintiff.
Steve Shasey, Esq. 415-623-7000 of Varela Lee Metz & Guarino REAL PARTY IN INTEREST DPR CONSTRUCTION.
Brooke Anderson,Esq. 415-484-8262 of Nixon Peabody for Defendant The Regenets of the University of California.

RULING - A R G U E D. Plaintiff argues. Real Party in Interest responds. (as fully reported in the reporter's notes/transcript) The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

REAL PARTY IN INTEREST DPR CONSTRUCTION, A GENERAL PARTNERSHIP's DEMURRER TO REDWOOD ELECTRIC GROUP, INC.'S PETITION FOR WRIT OF MANDATE AND COMPLAINT FOR DECLARATORY AND INJUNCTIVE RELIEF.

Real Party in Interest DPR Construction's demurrer to the third cause of action for declaratory and injunctive relief is sustained without leave to amend. (See Code Civ. Proc., 1061 ["The court may refuse to exercise the power granted by this chapter in any case where its declaration or determination is not necessary or proper at the time under all the circumstances."] The gravamen of the third cause of action contests respondent Regents' conduct during the course of the substitution administrative hearing. (Complaint, 90-95.) "It is settled that an action for declaratory relief is not appropriate to review an administrative decision." (State of California v. Superior Court (1974) 12 Cal.3d 237, 249; Tejon Real Estate, LLC v. City of Los Angeles (2014) 223 Cal.App.4th 149, 154-155 [declaratory relief proper only to declare statute unconstitutional on face, and not as applied to plaintiff by an administrative agency].) Declaratory relief also cannot be joined with a writ of mandate reviewing an administrative determination. (City of Pasadena v. Cohen (2014) 228 Cal.App.4th 1461, 1467; Guilbert v. Regents of University of California (1979) 93 Cal.App.3d 233, 244.) Plaintiff's declaratory relief claim runs afoul of this authority and it fails to show how the claim is necessary to accord complete relief. Plaintiff's reliance on Venice Town Council, Inc. v. City of Los Angeles (1996) 47 Cal.App.4th 1547 is misplaced. There, the court concluded that the declaratory relief claim was well-pleaded because the appellants did "not challenge any particular decision or order?Instead, appellants seek to resolve the City's fundamental misunderstanding of its responsibilities under the Mello Act to avoid continued violations or nonenforcement in the future. Declaratory relief has been held to be the proper remedy when it is alleged an agency has a policy of ignoring or violating applicable laws." (Id. at 1566.)

In addition, it appears that the issues that Plaintiff raises in its opposition regarding DPR's alleged violations of the Fair Practices Act can be resolved in the parties' companion litigation. (See CGC-19-577544.) In fact, that action presently includes allegations regarding DPR's alleged violations of the Fair Practices Act.

Order is signed. (see scanned order for details)

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: M. Goodman, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
